Member-only story

How to go from “I know what Strategy is” to “I know when to use it”

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Design Pattern Decision Map — visually tracing common software engineering complexities (orange) to their clean, architectural pattern solutions (blue)
Most engineers don’t fail at design patterns because they forgot what a Visitor is. They fail because they reach for a pattern at the wrong moment — or worse, apply one they don’t need at all.
The real challenge isn’t recall. It’s diagnosis.
A decision tree forces the discipline that textbooks skip: before you pick a pattern, you name the problem. That one step eliminates 80% of over-engineering.
Why patterns fail in practice
Patterns earn their place by reducing a recurring cost. That cost shows up in one of a few recognizable ways:

• Changes require touching too many files

• Tests are brittle because code has no clean seam

• External APIs leak into domain logic and spread translation code everywhere

• Constructors keep growing and valid combinations become unclear

• The same logic gets duplicated because there’s no stable home for it
Treating a pattern as an upgrade is the trap. It isn’t one. A pattern is a way of paying for flexibility…
